title: SEGMENT 10 — Organoid-on-chip: microfluidics and the vasculature problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Organoid-on-chip: microfluidics and the vasculature problem
Alright, welcome back.
We're going to spend the second half exploring some of the most exciting and most rapidly developing areas in the field.
Let's start with the technology that's most directly addressing the vasculature problem.
I mentioned in Segment 9 that organoids can't grow larger than a couple of millimeters in static culture because the interior runs out of oxygen.

The biological reason is that diffusion is too slow over distances larger than a couple hundred microns; living tissue, beyond that distance, needs active perfusion — fluid actually flowing through it — to deliver oxygen and nutrients.
The natural solution is to build the perfusion ourselves.
This is the idea behind organoid-on-chip systems — also called organ-on-a-chip when used with simpler cell cultures, or microphysiological systems in the more general framing.
The basic concept is a small device, typically made of clear silicone polymer, perhaps the size of a USB stick, with tiny channels carved into it.
Cells or organoids are seeded into chambers within the device, and culture medium is pumped through the channels at a controlled flow rate.
The flow brings nutrients and oxygen to the cells and carries away waste, just as blood does in your body.
The flow can also apply mechanical forces — shear stress on the cells lining the channels — which turns out to be biologically important for many cell types.
Vascular endothelial cells, for example, behave differently when they experience shear stress than when they're sitting in static medium.
So the chip isn't just providing perfusion; it's providing a more biologically realistic mechanical environment.

The first generation of organ-on-chip devices, pioneered by Don Ingber's group at the Wyss Institute at Harvard, were simpler than organoids — they typically had monolayers of cells (lung epithelial cells, gut epithelial cells, liver cells) arranged in two-layer chips with a porous membrane between two channels.
One channel might have epithelial cells, simulating the lining of an organ; the other channel might have endothelial cells, simulating a blood vessel.
Perfusion in both channels mimicked the physiological setup.
These early chips were valuable for studying transport between blood and tissue, drug absorption, infection biology, and other interface phenomena.
The newer generation combines chip devices with actual organoids.
You grow organoids using standard methods, then transfer them into a perfusion chamber on a chip, where they continue to grow under flow.
The flow extends the size at which organoids can survive, slows down necrotic core formation, and brings them closer to physiologic conditions.

Some groups are even building chips with engineered vascular channels lined with endothelial cells that connect to the organoid's surface, so that the perfusion happens through proper vessel-like structures rather than just through bulk medium flow.
The frontier here is vascularized organoids on chips.
You co-culture stem-cell-derived endothelial cells with the organoid's parenchymal cells, and as the organoid grows, the endothelial cells form proto-vessel networks within it.

When this organoid is placed on a chip with appropriate inflow and outflow channels, the chip's flow can be coaxed to enter the organoid's endogenous vessel network, perfusing the interior just as blood would perfuse a real tissue.
Some labs are getting this to work, in primitive form, with kidney and liver and brain organoids.
The results aren't yet at the point where you can grow a centimeter-scale organ, but they're substantially beyond what static culture can achieve.
We're moving toward the point where the size ceiling on organoid biology becomes a function of how clever your perfusion is, not a fundamental physical limit.
There's a related, even more ambitious frontier: the body-on-a-chip or multi-organ chip.

The idea is to connect several different organoids — say, a liver organoid, a kidney organoid, a heart organoid, and a gut organoid — on the same chip, with shared perfusion that mimics blood circulation.
You administer a drug to the system.
The gut organoid absorbs it.
The liver organoid metabolizes it.
The kidney organoid clears the metabolites.
The heart organoid experiences whatever cardiovascular effects the drug has.
All of this happens in a coupled system that models, in primitive form, how the drug would behave in a whole organism.
These systems are still experimental and the data they produce is still imperfect, but they offer a path toward replacing some of the animal studies that drug development currently requires.

The FDA has been increasingly receptive to organoid-on-chip data as part of drug submissions, and there's a 2022 law — the FDA Modernization Act 2.0 — that explicitly allows alternative methods like organoid testing to replace some required animal testing.
The regulatory framework is shifting in favor of these technologies.
The vasculature problem isn't solved.
But it's no longer a brick wall.
It's an engineering challenge being chipped away at, one perfusion design at a time.
